title: Rigid heddle bamboo-cotton scarf
subtitle: A plain weave scarf in a bamboo-cotton blend on a rigid heddle loom, approximately 160 x 22 cm
excerpt: A bamboo-cotton blend scarf is a good intermediate rigid heddle project: bamboo contributes natural drape and a soft sheen, cotton provides stability and washability, and the plain weave structure shows the blend well. The scarf changes noticeably after wet-finishing, softening and draping more freely than it does on the loom.
sourceNotes: Rigid heddle bamboo-cotton weaving guidance is consistent across UK guild instruction and rigid heddle loom manufacturer documentation. Wet-finishing behaviour of bamboo-cotton is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A weaving tool with alternating holes and slots through which warp threads are threaded. Lifting the heddle raises the slotted threads to form one shed; lowering it raises the holed threads to form the other. The two sheds alternate to create plain weave without separate shafts.

The number of warp ends per centimetre (or per inch). In rigid heddle weaving, the sett is determined by the dents-per-centimetre rating of the reed: a 10-dent reed produces 10 ends per 10 cm at one end per slot and hole. Bamboo-cotton DK typically weaves well at 8 to 10 ends per 10 cm.

The process of washing and pressing woven cloth after it is removed from the loom to set the weave, remove any sizing from the yarn, and achieve the final drape and dimensions. Most woven cloth shrinks and changes hand noticeably after wet-finishing and the finished measurements must account for this.

content.text: Bamboo-cotton blend yarn weaves beautifully on a
than an all-cotton yarn of the same weight. The looser sett allows the bamboo to drape freely after

and produces a scarf that hangs with a smooth, soft hand. The finished scarf before wet-finishing measures approximately 170 x 24 cm on the loom; after finishing it will be closer to 160 x 22 cm as the cloth relaxes and closes up slightly.

items.name: Bamboo-cotton DK blend yarn, main colour for warp and weft
Bamboo-cotton DK blend yarn, contrast colour for weft stripes (optional)
Rigid heddle loom, 8-dent or 10-dent reed, minimum 25 cm weaving width
Warping board for measuring warp length

content.text: Step 1: Calculate and wind the warp

Wind the warp on a warping board to a length of 210 cm: 170 cm for the scarf body, plus 40 cm for loom waste and fringes. For a 22 cm finished width at 8 ends per 10 cm on an 8-dent reed, wind approximately 180 ends. Transfer the warp to the loom and spread it evenly across the back beam before threading.

Thread the warp alternately through slots and holes across the full width of the heddle, one end per slot or hole. The ends in the slots will lift when the heddle is raised; the ends in the holes will lift when the heddle is lowered. Check that every end is under the same tension before tying on to the front beam. Bamboo-cotton yarn is slippery and the ends can shift during threading; check alignment carefully before weaving begins.

Weave 8 to 10 rows of tabby in a scrap yarn first to spread the warp evenly and check the selvedges before starting with the main yarn. Begin the scarf body: raise the heddle, pass the shuttle from right to left, then lower the heddle and return. Beat each row back with the heddle before opening the next shed. Bamboo-cotton does not require a firm beat; a moderate pressure keeps the weft density consistent without compressing the cloth. Weave for 170 cm (measure on the loom, which will relax to about 160 cm after finishing).

Cut the scarf off the loom, leaving at least 15 cm of warp ends for the fringe. Tie the warp ends in groups of 4 with overhand knots to hold the weave structure before washing. Hand wash in warm water using a gentle detergent and support the wet cloth fully; do not wring. Lay flat or hang to dry, then press with a warm iron under a damp cloth while slightly damp. The bamboo content will bloom and soften significantly.

items.problem: The warp ends keep slipping off the selvedge of the heddle during weaving
items.cause: Bamboo-cotton is more slippery than wool and the outer threads can work loose from the slots if tension is uneven
items.fix: Check that the outer warp threads are under the same tension as the interior threads. If the edge threads continue to slip, place a small piece of folded card under them in the slot to increase grip.

items.problem: The finished scarf is much shorter than expected after wet-finishing
items.cause: Bamboo-cotton can shrink up to 10 per cent on first washing, more if washed in hot water
items.fix: Always allow a 10 per cent shrinkage margin in the warp length calculation. Wet-finish in hand-warm water (not hot) and lay flat to dry rather than hanging, which stretches a wet bamboo-cotton cloth lengthways.
